_comment: Study notes. 'words' apply to every whole-word occurrence app-wide (case-insensitive). 'passages' apply to a specific verse and highlight a phrase within it. Keep notes neutral and evidentiary; cite sources. category: archaic | contested.

words.title: wot / wist
words.body: Archaic English from Old English witan, 'to know'. 'wot' = know (present); 'wist' = knew (past). E.g. “I wot not” = “I do not know.”

words.title: holpen
words.body: Archaic past participle of 'help' = helped (e.g. Psalm 86:17; Luke 1:54).

words.title: prevent (archaic: to precede)
words.body: In the KJV 'prevent' keeps its older sense from Latin praevenire, 'to come before, go ahead of, anticipate' — not the modern 'hinder/stop'. E.g. 1 Thessalonians 4:15, “shall not prevent them which are asleep” = shall not precede them.

words.title: conversation (archaic: conduct)
words.body: In the KJV 'conversation' means manner of life / conduct, not speech. It renders Greek anastrophe (G391) and politeuma (G4175). E.g. Philippians 1:27; 1 Peter 1:15.

words.title: suffer (often: allow/permit)
words.body: In the KJV 'suffer' frequently means 'allow, permit' (e.g. “Suffer the little children to come unto me,” Matthew 19:14), in addition to the modern sense of undergoing pain. Context decides which.

words.title: quick (archaic: living)
words.body: Archaic 'quick' = alive, living. E.g. “the quick and the dead” (Acts 10:42); “the word of God is quick, and powerful” (Hebrews 4:12).

words.title: meat (archaic: food)
words.body: In the KJV 'meat' means food of any kind, not specifically flesh. E.g. Genesis 1:29, herbs and fruit given “for meat.”

words.title: charity = love (agape)
words.body: 'Charity' renders Greek agape (G26) — the very same word translated 'love' elsewhere (e.g. John 3:16). The KJV uses 'charity' mainly in 1 Corinthians 13; the underlying word is identical to 'love'.

words.title: unicorn = wild ox (re'em)
words.body: Translates Hebrew re'em (H7214), a wild, untamable horned animal widely identified as the aurochs (wild ox). The mythical single-horned sense came later via the Greek Septuagint's 'monokeros' and the Latin 'unicornis'. E.g. Numbers 23:22; Job 39:9-10.

words.title: hell (several distinct words)
words.body: The KJV uses one word, 'hell', for several different originals: Hebrew Sheol (H7585, the grave/underworld), Greek Hades (G86), Gehenna (G1067, the valley of Hinnom), and once the verb tartaroo (G5020, 2 Peter 2:4). These are distinct concepts in the source texts.

words.title: baptize (transliteration)
words.body: 'Baptize' transliterates rather than translates Greek baptizo (G907), 'to immerse, dip, submerge.' The English word carries the Greek sound rather than its plain meaning.

words.title: church = assembly (ekklesia)
words.body: Translates Greek ekklesia (G1577), 'assembly, congregation, the called-out'. The same word is used of an ordinary civic assembly in Acts 19:39, 41.

words.title: Jew / Judah / Judea
words.body: Properly a member of the tribe or kingdom of Judah; afterward applied to any Israelite of the return from captivity, and to one who holds the religion of Israel. Hebrew Yehudi (H3064), of Yehudah (Judah); Greek Ioudaios (G2453), a Judean. Being at root tribal and territorial, the word is in many places equally rendered 'Judean.' The Idumeans — Edom, the seed of Esau — were subdued by John Hyrcanus about 125 B.C. and brought into Judaism by circumcision, and the house of Herod sprang from them (Josephus). Paul marks two senses: a Jew 'outwardly,' in the flesh, and a Jew 'inwardly,' of the heart (Romans 2:28-29). In the 1611 edition the word stood 'Iewes,' I and J being then one letter; 'Jew' is the later spelling. First found at 2 Kings 16:6.
sources: S. Mason, 'Jews, Judaeans, Judaizing, Judaism' (Journal for the Study of Judaism, 2007)

words.title: angel (messenger)
words.body: Hebrew mal'ak (H4397) and Greek angelos (G32) both mean 'messenger' - the same word is used for human messengers and for heavenly beings; context decides which. Angels are created spirits who serve God and minister to His people (Psalm 103:20; Hebrews 1:14).

words.title: archangel
words.body: Greek archangelos (G743), 'chief/ruling angel'. The word appears only twice: the Lord descends 'with the voice of the archangel' (1 Thessalonians 4:16), and Michael is called 'the archangel' (Jude 9).

words.title: Gabriel
words.body: Hebrew Gavri'el (H1403), 'man/strength of God'. An angel who stands in God's presence; he explains visions to Daniel (Daniel 8:16; 9:21) and announces the births of John the Baptist and Jesus (Luke 1:19, 26).

words.title: cherub / cherubims
words.body: Hebrew kerub (H3742); KJV plural 'cherubims'. Winged heavenly beings associated with God's throne and presence: set to guard Eden (Genesis 3:24), figured in gold over the mercy seat (Exodus 25:18-22), and seen in Ezekiel's visions (Ezekiel 10).

words.title: seraphim
words.body: Hebrew saraph (H8314), 'burning ones'; KJV 'seraphims'. Six-winged beings seen above the LORD's throne, calling 'Holy, holy, holy' - only in Isaiah 6:2-6.

words.title: Satan
words.body: Hebrew satan (H7854), 'adversary, accuser'; Greek Satanas (G4567). The accuser of God's people (Job 1-2; Zechariah 3:1-2) and the tempter (Matthew 4:10), identified in Revelation 12:9 with the devil and 'that old serpent'.

words.title: devil / devils
words.body: Greek diabolos (G1228), 'slanderer, accuser' — the Devil. The KJV uses 'devils' also for daimonion (G1140), 'demon'; so 'devil' may mean the Devil himself (diabolos) or a demon (daimonion).

words.title: Beelzebub
words.body: Greek Beelzeboul / Beelzebub (G954), called 'the prince of the devils' (Matthew 12:24). The name derives from Baal-zebub, 'lord of flies', the god of Ekron (2 Kings 1:2-3).

words.title: Belial
words.body: Hebrew beliyya'al (H1100), 'worthlessness, wickedness'. 'Sons/men of Belial' means worthless or wicked men; in 2 Corinthians 6:15 'Belial' stands opposite Christ as a name for Satan / utter wickedness.

words.title: Abaddon / Apollyon
words.body: Hebrew Abaddon (H11), 'destruction', and its Greek equivalent Apollyon (G623), 'destroyer' - the name of the angel of the bottomless pit (Revelation 9:11).

words.title: leviathan
words.body: Hebrew livyathan (H3882). A great, untamable sea creature God describes at length to Job (Job 41); He alone masters it (Psalm 104:26; Isaiah 27:1, where it pictures a fleeing serpent).

words.title: behemoth
words.body: Hebrew behemoth (H930). A massive land creature shown to Job as 'the chief of the ways of God' (Job 40:15-24).

words.title: giants (Nephilim / Rephaim)
words.body: In the KJV 'giants' translates two Hebrew words: nephilim (H5303) in Genesis 6:4, and rephaim (H7497). Other peoples - the Anakim, Emim, and Zamzummim - were reckoned among them (Numbers 13:33; Deuteronomy 2:10-11, 20-21); Og of Bashan 'remained of the remnant of the giants' (Deuteronomy 3:11).

words.title: saint = holy one
words.body: Hebrew qadosh (H6918) / chasid (H2623) and Greek hagios (G40), 'holy one, set-apart one'. In Scripture 'saints' are God's holy people - believers generally (Romans 1:7, 'called to be saints'; Psalm 16:3; Daniel 7:18) - not a separately canonized class.

words.title: legion
words.body: A Roman legion was an army unit of roughly 5,000-6,000 soldiers. The unclean spirits in the Gadarene man took the name to say 'we are many' (Mark 5:9, 15; Luke 8:30); Jesus likewise speaks of 'more than twelve legions of angels' (Matthew 26:53).

words.title: the angel of the LORD
words.body: Hebrew mal'ak YHWH, the messenger of the LORD. He appears to Hagar, to Abraham, to Moses at the bush, to Gideon and others; he speaks as God in the first person, and receives worship. Because of this he has long been understood as a manifestation of God Himself — by many Christian writers, the pre-incarnate Son — while some take him for a created messenger bearing God's name. Genesis 16:7-13; 22:11-18; Exodus 3:2-6; Judges 6:11-23.

words.title: the host of heaven
words.body: Hebrew tsaba ha-shamayim, 'the army of heaven'. It can mean the angelic armies around God's throne (1 Kings 22:19) or the sun, moon, and stars - which Israel was forbidden to worship (Deuteronomy 4:19; 2 Kings 17:16). Context decides which.

words.title: the LORD of hosts
words.body: Hebrew YHWH Tseba'oth, 'the LORD of armies' - God as commander of the heavenly host. A frequent title in the Prophets (Isaiah 6:3; 1 Samuel 17:45).

words.title: principalities & powers
words.body: In the New Testament 'principalities' (Greek arche, G746) heads a list of spiritual ranks - principalities, powers, thrones, dominions, mights - created through Christ (Colossians 1:16) and, fallen, the powers the believer wrestles against (Ephesians 6:12; Romans 8:38).

words.title: the sons of God
words.body: Hebrew bene ha-elohim. In Genesis 6:2,4 and Job 1:6; 2:1; 38:7 it denotes heavenly beings (read by some as angels, by others as the godly line of Seth). In the New Testament it also names those who belong to God by faith (John 1:12; Romans 8:14).

words.title: morning star / day star
words.body: Applied to more than one figure: the 'morning stars' that sing at creation (Job 38:7, heavenly beings); the 'day star/morning star' (helel) the KJV renders 'Lucifer', of the king of Babylon (Isaiah 14:12); and Jesus, 'the bright and morning star' (Revelation 22:16; cf. 2 Peter 1:19).

words.title: the living creatures (four beasts)
words.body: The four 'living creatures' (Hebrew chayyoth) of Ezekiel 1 and 10 - identified there as cherubim - and the 'four beasts' (Greek zoa) around God's throne in Revelation 4:6-9, each with four faces or many eyes, who ceaselessly worship.

words.title: scapegoat (Azazel)
words.body: Hebrew azazel (H5799): the goat sent into the wilderness on the Day of Atonement bearing the people's sins (Leviticus 16:8-10,26). 'Scapegoat' is Tyndale's coinage ('escape goat'); whether azazel is a place, the act of removal, or a name is debated.

words.title: Gog and Magog
words.body: Gog, of the land of Magog, leads a great hostile coalition that God judges in Ezekiel 38-39; 'Gog and Magog' reappear in Revelation 20:8 as the nations gathered against God's people at the end.

words.title: the watchers
words.body: Aramaic iyr (H5894), 'watcher' - a holy heavenly being in Nebuchadnezzar's vision, by whose decree ('the watchers... the holy ones') judgement is pronounced (Daniel 4:13,17,23).

words.title: Adam
words.body: Hebrew adam (H121), 'man' (also the word for mankind, H120). The first man, formed from the ground and set in Eden; through his disobedience sin and death entered the world (Genesis 2-5; Romans 5:12-19).

words.title: Eve
words.body: Hebrew Chavvah (H2332), 'living'. The first woman, made from Adam's side, 'the mother of all living' (Genesis 2:22; 3:20).

words.title: Noah
words.body: Hebrew Noach (H5146). A righteous man preserved with his family through the Flood in the ark; God's covenant with him is sealed by the rainbow (Genesis 6-9).

words.title: Abraham (Abram)
words.body: Hebrew Avraham (H85), 'father of a multitude' (formerly Abram). The patriarch called from Ur; father of Isaac through Sarah and Ishmael through Hagar; through him God covenants to bless all nations (Genesis 12-25; Romans 4).

words.title: Sarah (Sarai)
words.body: Hebrew Sarah (H8283), 'princess' (formerly Sarai). Wife of Abraham; bore Isaac in old age by promise (Genesis 17-21).

words.title: Isaac
words.body: Hebrew Yitschaq (H3327), 'he laughs'. Son of Abraham and Sarah, the child of promise; father of Esau and Jacob (Genesis 21-27).

words.title: Rebekah
words.body: Wife of Isaac and mother of the twins Esau and Jacob (Genesis 24-27).

words.title: Jacob (Israel)
words.body: Hebrew Yaaqob (H3290), renamed Israel ('he struggles with God'). Son of Isaac; father of the twelve tribal patriarchs (Genesis 25-50). ('Jacob' is also the name of Joseph's father in Matthew 1:16.)

words.title: Esau (Edom)
words.body: Hebrew Esav (H6215), also called Edom. Elder twin of Jacob who sold his birthright; ancestor of the Edomites (Genesis 25; 36).

words.title: Joseph
words.body: Several men share the name: Joseph son of Jacob, sold into Egypt and raised to power there (Genesis 37-50); Joseph the husband of Mary (Matthew 1); Joseph of Arimathaea, who buried Jesus (Matthew 27:57-60); and Joseph called Barsabbas (Acts 1:23).

words.title: Moses
words.body: Hebrew Mosheh (H4872). The prophet who led Israel out of Egypt, received the Law at Sinai, and wrote the Torah (Exodus - Deuteronomy).

words.title: Aaron
words.body: Hebrew Aharon (H175). Brother of Moses and Israel's first high priest (Exodus 28; Leviticus 8-9).

words.title: Miriam
words.body: Sister of Moses and Aaron; a prophetess who led Israel in song at the Red Sea (Exodus 15:20-21; Numbers 12).

words.title: Joshua
words.body: Hebrew Yehoshua (H3091), 'the LORD saves' - the same name rendered 'Jesus' in Greek. Moses' successor, who led Israel into Canaan (Joshua). (Also Joshua the high priest, Zechariah 3.)

words.title: Caleb
words.body: With Joshua, one of the two faithful spies who urged Israel to take the land; he later inherited Hebron (Numbers 13-14; Joshua 14).

words.title: Gideon
words.body: A judge who, with only 300 men, routed the Midianites (Judges 6-8).

words.title: Samson
words.body: Hebrew Shimshon (H8123). A Nazirite judge of great strength who fought the Philistines (Judges 13-16).

words.title: Ruth
words.body: A Moabite widow who clung to Naomi and married Boaz; great-grandmother of David and an ancestress of Christ (Ruth; Matthew 1:5).

words.title: Boaz
words.body: A kinsman-redeemer of Bethlehem who married Ruth (Ruth 2-4).

words.title: Samuel
words.body: Hebrew Shemuel (H8050). The last judge and a prophet, who anointed both Saul and David (1 Samuel).

words.title: Saul
words.body: Two men share the name: Saul son of Kish, Israel's first king, who fell from God's favour (1 Samuel 9-31); and Saul of Tarsus, the persecutor who became the apostle Paul (Acts 7:58; 13:9).

words.title: David
words.body: Hebrew David (H1732). Shepherd, psalmist, and Israel's greatest king; God promised his throne an everlasting heir (1-2 Samuel; Psalms; 2 Samuel 7). Jesus is called 'son of David'.

words.title: Solomon
words.body: Hebrew Shelomoh (H8010). Son of David; builder of the Temple, renowned for wisdom; wrote Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and the Song of Solomon (1 Kings 1-11).

words.title: Goliath
words.body: The Philistine giant of Gath whom the young David killed with a sling and stone (1 Samuel 17).

words.title: Elijah (Elias)
words.body: Hebrew Eliyyah (H452); Greek 'Elias'. A prophet who confronted Baal worship and was taken up in a whirlwind (1 Kings 17 - 2 Kings 2).

words.title: Elisha
words.body: Successor of Elijah, a prophet of many miracles (2 Kings 2-13).

words.title: Job
words.body: A blameless man of Uz, tested by great loss, who questions and finally trusts God (the book of Job).

words.title: Isaiah (Esaias)
words.body: Hebrew Yeshayah (H3470); Greek 'Esaias'. A major prophet of Judah who foretold the Messiah (Isaiah).

words.title: Jeremiah
words.body: The 'weeping prophet' who warned Judah before its fall to Babylon (Jeremiah; Lamentations).

words.title: Ezekiel
words.body: A priest-prophet among the Babylonian exiles, given visions of God's glory and of restoration (Ezekiel).

words.title: Daniel
words.body: A Judean exile in Babylon, famed for wisdom, faithfulness in the lions' den, and apocalyptic visions (Daniel).

words.title: Ezra
words.body: A priest and scribe who taught the Law to the returned exiles (Ezra; Nehemiah 8).

words.title: Nehemiah
words.body: Cupbearer to the Persian king who returned to rebuild Jerusalem's walls (Nehemiah).

words.title: Esther (Hadassah)
words.body: A Jewish queen of Persia who risked her life to save her people from destruction (Esther).

words.title: Mordecai
words.body: Esther's cousin and guardian, who exposed a plot against the king and was honoured (Esther).

words.title: Jonah (Jonas)
words.body: A reluctant prophet swallowed by a great fish, then sent to preach to Nineveh (Jonah; cf. Matthew 12:40).

words.title: Nebuchadnezzar
words.body: King of Babylon who destroyed Jerusalem and was humbled by God until he acknowledged Him (Daniel 1-4).

words.title: Melchizedek
words.body: Hebrew Malki-tsedeq (H4442), 'king of righteousness'. King of Salem and 'priest of the most high God' who blessed Abram (Genesis 14:18-20); a type of Christ's priesthood (Psalm 110:4; Hebrews 7).

words.title: Mary
words.body: Several women share the name: Mary the mother of Jesus (Luke 1-2); Mary Magdalene (Luke 8:2; John 20); Mary of Bethany, sister of Martha and Lazarus (John 11-12); Mary the mother of James (Mark 16:1); and Mary the wife of Cleophas (John 19:25).

words.title: John
words.body: Several men share the name: John the Baptist, the forerunner of Christ (Luke 1; 3); John the apostle, son of Zebedee, 'the beloved disciple' (Matthew 4:21; John 21); and John Mark (Acts 12:12).

words.title: Peter (Cephas)
words.body: Simon, surnamed Peter / Cephas ('a stone'), a Galilean fisherman and a leading apostle (Matthew 16:16-18; the Gospels; Acts; 1-2 Peter).

words.title: Simon
words.body: Several men: Simon Peter the apostle; Simon the Zealot (Canaanite), another apostle; Simon of Cyrene, who carried Jesus' cross (Mark 15:21); Simon the leper (Mark 14:3); and Simon Magus the sorcerer (Acts 8).

words.title: James
words.body: Several men: James son of Zebedee, brother of John and an apostle (martyred, Acts 12:2); James son of Alphaeus, an apostle; and James the Lord's brother, leader of the Jerusalem church and writer of the epistle (Acts 15; James 1:1).

words.title: Paul
words.body: Formerly Saul of Tarsus; a Pharisee who persecuted the church until the risen Christ met him on the Damascus road, then the apostle to the Gentiles and writer of much of the New Testament (Acts 9; the Epistles).

words.title: Judas
words.body: Several men: Judas Iscariot, who betrayed Jesus (Matthew 26:14-16); Judas (Thaddaeus), an apostle (Luke 6:16); and Judas the Lord's brother, writer of the epistle of Jude (Matthew 13:55; Jude 1).

words.title: Herod
words.body: A ruling dynasty, not one man: Herod the Great, who sought to kill the infant Jesus (Matthew 2); Herod Antipas, who beheaded John the Baptist and tried Jesus (Mark 6; Luke 23); and Herod Agrippa I, who killed James (Acts 12).

words.title: Pontius Pilate
words.body: Pontius Pilate, the Roman governor of Judea who, against his own verdict, sentenced Jesus to crucifixion (Matthew 27; John 18-19).

words.title: Thomas
words.body: An apostle, called Didymus ('twin'), remembered for doubting then confessing the risen Christ: 'My Lord and my God' (John 20:24-29).

words.title: Matthew (Levi)
words.body: A tax collector (also called Levi) called to be an apostle; by tradition the writer of the first Gospel (Matthew 9:9).

words.title: Philip
words.body: Two men: Philip the apostle (John 1:43-46); and Philip the evangelist, one of the seven, who preached in Samaria and to the Ethiopian official (Acts 6:5; 8).

words.title: Stephen
words.body: One of the seven, and the first Christian martyr, stoned after his testimony before the council (Acts 6-7).

words.title: Barnabas
words.body: A Levite surnamed 'son of consolation', companion of Paul on the first missionary journey (Acts 4:36; 13-15).

words.title: Timothy
words.body: Paul's young coworker and 'son in the faith', recipient of two epistles (Acts 16:1; 1-2 Timothy).

words.title: Lazarus
words.body: Two figures: Lazarus of Bethany, whom Jesus raised from the dead (John 11); and Lazarus the beggar in Jesus' parable (Luke 16:19-31).

words.title: Martha
words.body: Sister of Mary and Lazarus of Bethany, who served while Mary listened (Luke 10:38-42; John 11).

words.title: Nicodemus
words.body: A Pharisee and ruler of the Jews who came to Jesus by night and later helped bury Him (John 3; 19:39).

passages.phrase: say they are Jews, and are not
passages.title: “say they are Jews, and are not”
passages.body: 'Them which say they are Jews, and are not' — Ioudaios (G2453). The claim is to the name; the Lord calls the claimants 'the synagogue of Satan.' Compare Paul's two senses of 'Jew,' the outward in the flesh and the inward of the heart (Romans 2:28-29).

passages.phrase: say they are Jews, and are not
passages.title: “say they are Jews, and are not”
passages.body: 'Which say they are Jews, and are not' — Ioudaios (G2453), as at Revelation 2:9, and again 'the synagogue of Satan.' Spoken to the church at Philadelphia. Compare the outward and inward 'Jew' of Romans 2:28-29.

passages.phrase: Ye are of your father the devil
passages.title: “your father the devil”
passages.body: Spoken to opponents among the Ioudaioi in the temple. 'Ye are of your father the devil' stands against their claim to Abraham and to God as Father (8:39-47): they do the devil's works. Whether the fatherhood meant is moral or of descent has been read both ways. The same chapter speaks of 'the Jews which believed on him' (8:31).

passages.title: Easter / Passover
passages.body: The underlying Greek is pascha (G3957), the ordinary word for 'Passover', which the KJV translates 'Passover' in its 28 other occurrences. Only here is it rendered 'Easter'. Tyndale's 1526 New Testament had 'ester' here.

passages.title: Lucifer
passages.body: Hebrew helel (H1966), 'shining one / morning star'. 'Lucifer' is the Latin Vulgate's word for the morning star (light-bearer); it is a description, not a proper name. The passage is an oracle against the king of Babylon (Isaiah 14:4).

passages.phrase: the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost
passages.title: Comma Johanneum
passages.body: The words 'the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost: and these three are one' (the Comma Johanneum) are wanting in the Greek manuscripts before the fourteenth century and in the early versions, and entered the printed Greek text late.
sources: Metzger, A Textual Commentary on the Greek NT

passages.phrase: he appeared first to Mary Magdalene
passages.title: Mark's longer ending (16:9-20)
passages.body: The last twelve verses of Mark (16:9-20) are wanting in the oldest Greek copies, Sinaiticus and Vaticanus, and are marked as doubtful or omitted in other witnesses.

passages.phrase: every man went unto his own house
passages.title: Pericope Adulterae (John 7:53-8:11)
passages.body: The account of the woman taken in adultery (John 7:53-8:11) is wanting in the oldest manuscripts and stands at differing places in others.

passages.title: Godhead (Colossians 2:9)
passages.body: Here 'Godhead' renders Greek theotes (G2320), 'deity, divine nature'. The KJV also prints 'Godhead' for two different Greek words: theios (G2304) in Acts 17:29 and theiotes (G2305) in Romans 1:20.

passages.title: let (archaic: to hinder)
passages.body: Archaic 'let' means to hinder, restrain, or hold back — the opposite of its modern sense. “He who now letteth will let” = he who now restrains will restrain. Compare Romans 1:13, “was let hitherto.”

passages.title: Michael
passages.body: Hebrew Miyka'el (H4317), 'who is like God'. An archangel and 'great prince' who stands guard over God's people (Daniel 10:13, 21; 12:1); he disputes with the devil over the body of Moses (Jude 9) and leads heaven's armies against the dragon (Revelation 12:7). ('Michael' is also an ordinary personal name elsewhere in the KJV.)

passages.title: Michael
passages.body: Hebrew Miyka'el (H4317), 'who is like God'. An archangel and 'great prince' who stands guard over God's people (Daniel 10:13, 21; 12:1); he disputes with the devil over the body of Moses (Jude 9) and leads heaven's armies against the dragon (Revelation 12:7). ('Michael' is also an ordinary personal name elsewhere in the KJV.)

passages.title: Michael
passages.body: Hebrew Miyka'el (H4317), 'who is like God'. An archangel and 'great prince' who stands guard over God's people (Daniel 10:13, 21; 12:1); he disputes with the devil over the body of Moses (Jude 9) and leads heaven's armies against the dragon (Revelation 12:7). ('Michael' is also an ordinary personal name elsewhere in the KJV.)

passages.title: Michael
passages.body: Hebrew Miyka'el (H4317), 'who is like God'. An archangel and 'great prince' who stands guard over God's people (Daniel 10:13, 21; 12:1); he disputes with the devil over the body of Moses (Jude 9) and leads heaven's armies against the dragon (Revelation 12:7). ('Michael' is also an ordinary personal name elsewhere in the KJV.)

passages.title: the dragon / that old serpent
passages.body: Revelation 12:9 draws the titles together: 'the great dragon... that old serpent, called the Devil, and Satan, which deceiveth the whole world' - linking the serpent of Eden (Genesis 3), the dragon, the Devil (diabolos), and Satan (the adversary). In the Old Testament 'dragon' often renders tannin, a sea-monster or serpent.
